claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit. To do this properly, I'll need to gather some information about your circumstances and any children you're responsible for.

Let me start with a few basic questions:

**Do you have any children you'd like to claim Child Benefit for? If so, could you tell me their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to claim for my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you'd like to claim Child Benefit for your son Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is 16 or over, there are specific rules that apply. Let me ask you:

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** For example, is he in school, college, university, or an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school but hasn't started anything else yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since Alex is 17 and not currently in approved education or training, there may still be a way for you to continue receiving Child Benefit.

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with any careers service or the armed forces. He's just been at home since leaving school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Thank you for that information.

Before I make my final assessment, I need to confirm one more important detail:

**Are you living with Alex in the UK, or are you making financial contributions towards his upkeep (such as food, clothes, pocket money, or housing)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester actually. And yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. I have all the information I need to assess your eligibility.

Based on the rules for Child Benefit, let me explain the situation:

Alex is 17 years old and no longer in approved education or training. According to the Child Benefit rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training **and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services.**

Since Alex has not registered with either of these services, he unfortunately falls outside the eligibility criteria for continued Child Benefit payments after leaving full-time education.

However, it's worth noting that if Alex does register with a careers service or the armed forces in the future, you could potentially claim for a 20-week period from that point.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**. 

The key issue is that while you live with Alex and are responsible for him, he is 17 and no longer in approved education or training. The only way to extend eligibility at this age is if he registers with a careers service or the armed forces, which he hasn't done yet.

If Alex does decide to register with a government-sponsored careers service or join the armed forces in the future, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for a 20-week period from that point. I'd recommend discussing this option with him.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has left full-time education or training. According to Child Benefit rules for 16-19 year olds, eligibility continues if the child stays in approved education or training. If they leave, you can claim for 20 weeks only if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex has not registered with either service, so he does not meet the eligibility criteria.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with Alex and is responsible for him. However, Alex is 17, has left full-time education, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed forces, which are the conditions required to extend Child Benefit eligibility beyond the end of full-time education. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex."}

If they leave, you can claim for 20 weeks only if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex has not registered with either service, so he does not meet the eligibility criteria.
